metadata.title: 80 Days to Stay — Progress Report: Building the OPT Job Search Platform

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A raw 30,370-row CSV is not usable for most international students in its current form. One statistic emerging from the data is pivotal to the project's central thesis. The cross-source entity mapping between SEC, DOL, and USCIS records is now complete. Good progress on data extraction sets up the next step of making the information searchable and useful.

artifactLines: A raw 30,370-row CSV is not usable for most international students in its current form.
One statistic emerging from the data is pivotal to the project's central thesis.
The cross-source entity mapping between SEC, DOL, and USCIS records is now complete.
Good progress on data extraction sets up the next step of making the information searchable and useful.

beats.narration_text: The pivot from a raw 30,370-row CSV that most international students can't use, toward completed cross-source entity mapping across SEC, DOL, and USCIS, tells me the value is in the joins. I want to understand the pipeline. Can you (1) explain what entity mapping across three government sources actually resolves, using one example employer, (2) describe how you'd turn the 30,370-row CSV into something an OPT job seeker can query, and (3) name the pivotal statistic this mapping is meant to prove and how you'd validate it?

PROF BEAR’S NOTE: Good progress on the data extraction
This gap is not a reflection of skill or effort
